topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope sat on my desk like a ticking time bomb. It was thick, cream-colored, and bore the unmistakable, elegant script of a formal notice. My hands were shaking so badly I had to grip the edge of the wooden surface to steady myself. Inside, the words were stark: "Relocation confirmed. New address: Denver, Colorado." My breath hitched, a shallow, painful thing in my chest. I reread the sentence three times, the ink blurring slightly under the pressure of my gaze. How could this be? Liam. My Liam. He was moving across the country. The silence of my apartment suddenly felt deafening, amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears. I slowly lowered the paper, the sound of the rustling paper seeming impossibly loud in the quiet room. I needed to breathe, but the air felt thick and heavy, refusing to settle.

[story 2]

I stared at the grainy photo on my phone—Liam grinning wildly on a beach trip last summer—and then back at the official-looking notification. A cold wash of nausea swept over me. It wasn't just a change of scenery; it was an amputation. My jaw tightened until my teeth ached. I walked to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, trying to anchor myself to the familiar view of the city skyline. Every familiar corner, every shared inside joke, suddenly felt tainted, overlaid with the stark reality of distance. When I finally turned back, my eyes scanned the room wildly, searching for an escape route that didn't exist. My throat felt constricted, a dry, tight knot forming where I wanted to swallow, but no sound came out. I just stood there, rooted to the spot, feeling utterly hollowed out.

[story 3]

When Sarah slid the printed letter across the coffee table, the casual gesture felt like a physical blow. "So, you're actually going?" I managed, the sound coming out thin and reedy. I picked up the paper, my fingers tracing the bolded destination. The world seemed to tilt on its axis. I could feel a strange, buzzing energy radiating from my fingertips, a frantic need to move, to shout, to somehow rewind the last few minutes. My gaze darted around the room, landing on every shared trinket, every memory tied to Liam, and the weight of it pressed down on my shoulders, making it impossible to draw a full breath. I managed a weak, humorless laugh, a sound that dissolved instantly into a shaky exhale.
